description:
Ran a backup last night off our PostgreSQL replica — 47 tables should've dumped to /home/user/backup/tables/, one sql.gz per table. Restore test this morning is failing on maybe a third of them, gunzip throws "unexpected end of file". Thing is the job reported success and the file sizes look reasonable, nothing truncated to zero.

    Best guess is something interrupted gzip mid-stream but the wrapper script kept going? Or maybe the parallel jobs clobbered each other writing to the same files somehow. Logs are at /home/user/backup/backup.log if that helps.

    Need the corrupted dumps recovered to valid gzip that pg can actually restore. Whatever's salvageable — I know some might be truly gone. Don't re-dump from the DB, replica's already been promoted and the data's changed.
truth:
Initial state:
    - /home/user/backup/tables/ contains 47 files named {tablename}.sql.gz
    - 31 files are valid gzip archives containing complete SQL dump data
    - 16 files are corrupted in a specific way: they contain TWO concatenated gzip streams where the second stream is truncated mid-deflate-block. This happened because the backup script used `gzip >>` append mode and a monitoring process separately gzipped partial progress snapshots to the same files, then got killed
    - The corruption pattern: [valid complete gzip stream A][partial gzip stream B with truncated deflate block]
    - gzip/gunzip fail because they try to decompress stream B after stream A and hit the truncation
    - The FIRST gzip stream in each corrupted file is actually complete and contains the full table dump — stream B was just garbage progress data that shouldn't have been there
    - File sizes appear normal because stream A is the bulk of the data
    - /home/user/backup/backup.log shows interleaved "DUMP: tablename started" / "DUMP: tablename complete" / "PROGRESS: tablename checkpoint" messages — the PROGRESS lines correlate with corrupted files
    - Tools available: python3, gzip, zlib (python), gunzip, xxd, hexdump, file, binwalk
    - postgresql-client is installed but the database connection would fail (replica promoted, credentials rotated)
    - /home/user/backup/ is fully writable

    Expected final state:
    - All 47 files in /home/user/backup/tables/ are valid gzip archives (gunzip -t passes on each)
    - The 31 originally valid files are byte-identical to their initial state
    - The 16 corrupted files have been repaired: they now contain ONLY the first (complete) gzip stream, with the corrupted second stream removed
    - `for f in /home/user/backup/tables/*.sql.gz; do gunzip -t "$f" || echo "FAIL: $f"; done` produces no FAIL lines
    - Each repaired file, when decompressed, contains valid SQL starting with "-- PostgreSQL dump" and ending with "-- Dump complete" or similar valid dump footer

    Invariants:
    - No files deleted from /home/user/backup/tables/
    - The 31 valid files unchanged (byte-identical)
    - No network/database access used (would fail anyway, but must not attempt)
    - Original corrupted files may be backed up elsewhere but the .sql.gz files must be the repaired versions

    Anti-shortcut guards:
    - Cannot simply `gunzip` and `gzip` — gunzip fails on corrupted files
    - Cannot use `gzip -d --force` alone — it produces truncated output when it hits the bad stream
    - Cannot delete corrupted files — all 47 must remain
    - The fix requires understanding gzip stream structure: finding where the first stream's valid trailer (CRC32 + ISIZE) ends and truncating there, or using zlib's decompressobj to read exactly one stream
    - Naive approaches like "skip bad bytes" or "use zcat 2>/dev/null" will either fail entirely or produce truncated SQL missing the dump footer
    - `grep -l "Dump complete" <(zcat file.sql.gz)` on repaired files must succeed for all 47 tables
name: task_000000_6dfd901e
